judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports fully and correctly answer all three questions with equivalent grounding; they are comparable.

report_1.evidence: `cedarline/.env` sets `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`, and `backend/app/db.py` builds the single SQLAlchemy engine straight from that variable... it also owns the tables created by `db/migrations/0001_init.sql`. Postgres exempts both superusers and table owners from RLS... it never runs `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`... the policy is simply never evaluated for this role — no error, no warning

report_2.evidence: The application connects to Postgres as the table owner, so row-level security is silently bypassed. `cedarline/.env` sets `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`... so it owns the `clients` and `appointments` tables. In Postgres, RLS policies do not apply to a table's owner (or superuser) unless the table has `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` — and `0001_init.sql` only does `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`, not `FORCE`

report_1.evidence: change `DATABASE_URL` in `.env` (and `.env.example`) to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar` — the role created by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which has only `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` grants and no ownership... keep a strict two-role split: `cedar_owner` used **only** by `scripts/migrate.sh` for schema changes... and `cedar_app` for everything the app does at runtime

report_2.evidence: Point the application's `DATABASE_URL` at the non-owner role: `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`... keeping `cedar_owner` solely for migrations... two roles — a privileged owner/migration role used only by migration tooling, and a least-privilege app role (DML only, no ownership) used by all runtime code

report_1.evidence: `cedar_app` is created only by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which the README and `docs/local-setup.md` describe as a **manual, one-time, first-setup** step — it is not in `db/migrations/` and not invoked by any script. `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, which deletes the `cedar_pgdata` volume... then brings the container back up and runs only `./scripts/migrate.sh`... the test suite... fails immediately with "role \"cedar_app\" does not exist"

report_2.evidence: `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, which deletes the `cedar_pgdata` volume — that destroys the entire Postgres cluster, including all roles... The `cedar_app` role is created by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which per its own comment, `docs/local-setup.md`, and the README is a "one-time" manual step outside the migrations. So after a reset the freshly initialized cluster has `cedar_owner`... but no `cedar_app`... To make it work again the teammate must re-run the bootstrap
